title: Matariki

sources.label: Te Papa: What and who is Matariki?
Te Papa: The stars of Matariki
NZ Government: Public holidays and anniversary dates
NASA: Interstellar Cloud in the Pleiades

review_notes: Add local iwi guidance where possible.
Do not present one Matariki model as universal for all iwi and regions.
No AI-generated cultural imagery was used.

slides.title: Matariki
slides.subtitle: The Māori New Year: a time to look to the sky, remember people, gather together, and look ahead.

slides.title: Connect a star cluster to people, place, and time

cards.title: Notice
cards.text: What is Matariki in the night sky?

cards.title: Explain
cards.text: Why does its winter rising matter?

cards.title: Connect
cards.text: How do remembrance, celebration, and hopes shape the New Year?

slides.callout: Learning intention: explain why Matariki is important and describe at least two ways it connects people with the natural world.

slides.title: A star cluster, not a single star
slides.lead: Matariki is the Māori name for the Pleiades star cluster, which is associated with the Māori New Year in Aotearoa New Zealand.

bullets.title: Astronomy
bullets.text: The cluster is also known as Messier 45.

bullets.title: Season
bullets.text: Its winter return marks a new cycle for many Māori.

bullets.title: Culture
bullets.text: Traditions vary by iwi and region.

slides.title: The cluster is visible as a group of stars
slides.lead: The Pleiades/Matariki cluster contains many stars, though only a handful are visible without a telescope.
slides.caption: NASA image detail: interstellar cloud in the Pleiades.
slides.source_note: Source: NASA Science image detail page.

slides.title: Remember, celebrate, look ahead

cards.title: Remembrance
cards.text: Honour those who have died since the last rising of Matariki.

cards.title: Celebration
cards.text: Gather with others and give thanks for what we have.

cards.title: Future
cards.text: Set hopes and intentions for the new year.

slides.note: Classroom wording: What do people remember? What do people celebrate? What do people hope for?
slides.source_note: Source: Mānawatia a Matariki About page.

slides.title: The cluster becomes a map of relationships
slides.lead: The stars invite learners to connect astronomy with water, weather, food, memory, wellbeing, and future hopes.

slides.title: One common learning model names nine stars
slides.lead: Each whetū can connect to a part of life.

items.title: Waitī
items.text: fresh water and life within it

slides.source_note: Source: Te Papa “The stars of Matariki”.

slides.title: Not every community marks Matariki in the same way
slides.lead: Some iwi traditions focus on Puanga or Puaka rather than Matariki. Some traditions describe seven visible stars; others teach nine.

cards.title: Matariki
cards.text: The Pleiades cluster is used by many Māori communities to mark the New Year.

cards.title: Puanga / Puaka
cards.text: Some communities use another star, known elsewhere as Rigel, as the seasonal marker.

slides.callout: Teacher move: ask, “What is the local iwi context for our place?”
slides.source_note: Source: Te Papa regional variations page.

slides.title: Make meaning from the cluster

steps.label: Observe
steps.title: Look carefully
steps.text: What do you notice in the real star-cluster photo?

steps.label: Connect
steps.title: Choose one whetū
steps.text: How does its meaning connect to your community or environment?

steps.label: Explain
steps.title: Use because
steps.text: “Matariki matters because...” Include sky evidence and cultural meaning.

slides.note: Optional: students create a one-page star card with name, meaning, picture, and one respectful question.

slides.title: Use care when teaching cultural knowledge
slides.lead: This deck is an introduction, not a replacement for local voices. Treat iwi variation as part of the learning, not as an error.

cards.title: Keep
cards.text: Keep sources visible and add local context where possible.

cards.title: Avoid
cards.text: Avoid claiming one universal version for all iwi and regions.

slides.callout: Classroom review status: pending local-context review.

slides.title: Show your understanding
prompts: One respectful question I still have is...
slides.challenge: Challenge: explain how one event can be both astronomical and cultural.

slides.title: Sources used
slides.note: Image policy: real astronomy visuals only; no AI-generated cultural imagery. Cultural detail should be reviewed against local iwi context before classroom use.
